nativeRange.habitat: Blackwater streams and pools associated with peat swamps, heavily shaded by forest canopy. The water is extremely soft and acidic, stained brown by tannins released from decomposing leaf litter on the forest floor.

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on zooplankton and small invertebrates. In captivity, generally accepts high-quality micro foods such as crushed flakes, micro pellets, frozen or live baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, micro worms, and rotifers. Food must be very small due to their tiny mouths.

summary: The Chili Rasbora (Boraras brigittae) is one of the smallest aquarium fish in the hobby, reaching just 1.5 to 2 cm as an adult. Native to Borneo's blackwater peat swamps, it thrives in soft, acidic water and tends to show its best coloration and confidence when kept in groups of eight or more.
careNotes: The Chili Rasbora is one of the smallest aquarium fish available, reaching only around 1.5 to 2 cm as an adult. The species is endemic to southwestern Borneo (South Kalimantan), where it inhabits ancient forest peat swamps with extremely soft, acidic blackwater. Water chemistry matters considerably: the Chili Rasbora tends to be sensitive to high pH and hard water, so soft, acidic conditions are generally advised and the use of a well-established, biologically mature tank is strongly recommended before introduction. Dense planting with species such as Java moss and floating plants, along with leaf litter and driftwood, helps recreate the blackwater biotope and provides cover for these naturally timid fish. Low to moderate lighting is preferred. Juveniles appear pale and may take several weeks to develop their characteristic scarlet coloration as they settle into their new environment.
breedingNotes: Breeding the Chili Rasbora is considered moderately difficult. The species is a continuous egg-scatterer with no parental care, and adults will readily consume their own eggs, so a separate breeding tank fitted with a fine mesh bottom or a thick layer of Java moss is generally advised to protect the eggs from predation. In good conditions the fish tend to spawn daily, with eggs hatching in approximately 48 hours. The fry are very small and require microscopic live foods such as infusoria or paramecium in their first days, graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro worms after roughly 7 to 10 days. Males tend to be smaller with brighter red coloration, while females appear noticeably rounder when carrying eggs.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary reference for taxonomy, native range, water parameters, and husbandry. Used as primary due to depth of scientific and husbandry detail.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-check for diet, temperament, tankmates, and general care. pH listed as 6.0–8.0, which is broader and less ecologically accurate for this blackwater species than the 4.0–7.0 range confirmed by other sources.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-check for taxonomy, size, and water parameters. pH listed as 6.5–7.0 which appears conservative for a blackwater species; temperature and hardness broadly consistent with other sources.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-check for lifespan (4–5 years), pH (4.0–7.0 confirmed), and breeding notes. kH listed as 3–12 dKH which seems high for a blackwater species; 0–2 used based on habitat ecology and SeriouslyFish data.

fish.breedingNotes: Continuous egg-scatterer with no parental care; adults will eat their own eggs. A separate breeding tank with a mesh floor or dense fine-leaved moss is generally advised to protect the eggs. Fry require microscopic live foods such as infusoria or paramecium initially.
